data.title: Weibo Corporation: Still Long Even Though Headwinds Have Gained Strength
data.description:
Weibo Corporation (WB), a social media platform in China, has not done well recently, despite the release of the latest earnings report during this time. The Q4 FY2025 report came in below expectations, which caused the stock to drop and pushed it below a support level that had kept WB afloat for months. However, while headwinds have gotten stronger for several reasons, there are nonetheless plenty of reasons to be long WB.

WB fell below a key support level

A previous article from January 2026 written by myself mentioned why WB had enough to take it higher. For instance, the stock had rebounded after a selloff, which did not happen by accident at it occurred close to an important support level, around $9.50 or so. WB appeared to be heading higher by resuming the prior uptrend after a temporary correction, which is part of why I chose to rate WB a buy in the article.

However, this did not turn out to be correct because the stock proceeded to challenge the previously mentioned support level once more and while support did manage to repeat what it had done before, which is to prevent WB from going lower, by doing so for a second time, it was unable to do so for a third time. The chart above shows how the stock fell below the support level on March 18 after bouncing off of the support level multiple times.

A new support level seems to have been found

The previous article mentioned how WB had rallied from a low of $7.10 in April 2025 to a high of $12.96 in October 2025 and the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of $7.10 to $12.96 is $9.34 per ADS. WB challenged this support level a number of times in the past half year and for a time support was able to stand its ground.

For instance, he first challenge ended in November 2025 with WB reaching as low as $9.46, or just $0.12 above $9.34, before turning higher. Another challenge in the early part of March saw the stock hit an intraday low of $9.23 on March 9, which is below $9.34, only to bounce and close the day higher at $9.60, or above $9.34.

However, support at $9.34 did not hold because the stock was able to break below it on March 18 after the release of the latest report, which quickly resulted in a new 2026 low of $8.36 on March 19. WB has been dithering since then with no new lows and there seems to be a reason for this. The next Fibonacci retracement level following the 61.8% level is the 76.4% retracement level and this level seems to have come into play.

The 76.4% retracement of $7.10 to $12.96 is $8.48. This could explain why even though WB hit an intraday low of $8.36 on March 19, which is below $8.48, it bounced to close the day higher at $8.78, or above $8.48. The stock has been hovering above this price point in recent days, but it has not been able to close below $8.48, at least so far. WB closed at $8.58 per ADS on March 27.

All this suggests WB has found another support level. Furthermore, it is worth mentioning that after the recent slide in the stock, WB is now well into oversold territory with an RSI reading in the mid twenties. These conditions, oversold and close to support, could enable WB to regain its footing, perhaps to the point that it can go on another rally.

So while bulls suffered a setback with the loss of an important support level, which had kept the stock afloat for months, bulls can take solace in the fact that WB has found another support level to replace the one previously lost. It is not impossible for WB to break support once again, but the existence of support should provide a certain level of assurance for those thinking of getting in on WB.

A disappointing report breaks support

It’s no coincidence support was breached after the release of the most recent or Q4 FY2025 report. First, WB missed earnings expectations when it announced non-GAAP earnings of $0.25 per ADS, $0.07 below consensus estimates, on revenue of $473.26M in Q4 FY2025. In comparison, WB earned $0.40 per ADS on revenue of $456.83M in Q4 FY2024.

In terms of GAAP, which includes a number of items left out by non-GAAP, WB posted a net loss attributable to shareholders of $4.72M or $0.02 per ADS, worse than the net profit of $8.87M or $0.04 per ADS a year ago. Note that the loss was mostly due to losses on investments and not on operations. Remember also that earnings in the final quarter of the fiscal tends to be lower as the end of the year brings certain accounting adjustments that need to be done before wrapping up the fiscal.

In all of FY2025, non-GAAP earnings totaled $1.65 per ADS on revenue of $1,757.22M, which is worse than in FY2024 when WB earned $1.82 on revenue of $1,754.68M. In terms of GAAP, though, WB earned $1.70 per ADS in FY2025, which is more than the $1.16 earned in FY2024, but this is primarily due to income from interest and investments.

Income from operations, which looks at the core business and leaves out changes on the non-operating side, declined from $494.32M in FY2024 to $464.83M in FY2025. Still, WB continues to be a quite profitable business with gross margin of 76% and operating margin of 26.2%. WB finished FY2025 with total debt of $1.86B, but this was more than offset by cash and cash equivalents totaling $2.3B.

The market was likely also disappointed by WB reducing its dividend. WB announced a cash dividend of $0.61 per ADS for the year along with the latest report. This is less than prior years when WB paid $0.82, $0.82 and $0.85 in 2025, 2024 and 2023, respectively, per ADS. On the other hand, the dividend still represents a very respectable yield of 7.1% with the stock last closing at $8.58, which compares very well in the tech space.

Valuations are lower

WB has more in its corner than just a higher-than-average dividend yield and an oversold stock close to support. The decrease in the stock price, which has seen the stock go from a high of $12.96 in October to $8.58 most recently, a decrease of about a third or so in six months, has lowered valuations to the point that getting in on WB is now significantly more palatable in terms of multiples.

For instance, TTM non-GAAP EPADS of $1.65 translates to a P/E ratio of 5.2x for WB, which compares well in the social media space where P/E ratios tend to be much higher. Valuations are also low in terms of the balance sheet. WB has a book value of $16.39 per ADS, which means WB is available at less than book value with a price-to-book of 0.52x.

These multiples are well below what WB has tended to go for over the years. For instance, the average P/E in the last five years is 7.8x, well above the current 5.2x. Similarly, being valued at less than book value is unusual because on average WB has traded above it in the last five years. In other words, there is a sale going on.

What WB needs to work on

However, while there are reasons to be bullish on WB, which includes the stock close to support, multiples below the average and an above-average dividend yield, potential buyers of the stock may still be worried by the possibility earnings could come under further pressure and decrease like it did in the latest report.

For instance, while revenue grew, if only by a modest amount, operating income was reduced in both the final quarter and the year as a whole. This is because while revenue grew, cost of revenue grew even faster, as did most operating expenses. The latter includes product development, sales and marketing, but not general and administrative expenses.

WB needs to at the very least halt any decrease in earnings, and ideally grow it. To do this sustainably, revenue needs to grow faster than it has. WB needs more engagement from the users on the platform and ideally more users on the platform. To do this, WB has to come up with ways that gives people an incentive to turn to the platform and spend time on it.

It is worth mentioning that WB has been making a lot of changes in terms of how it operates internally, and these investments are in all likelihood driving up costs. For instance, WB has been working on search powered by AI and it is known from companies like Google that AI-enhanced search is more computationally intensive than regular search, which translates into higher cost.

The same is true for the recommendation engine WB has turned to in order to emulate the success companies like ByteDance have achieved with their recommendation engine. An interest-based feed using a recommendation engine will require more computations than a more traditional relationship-based feed, which means more spending on related infrastructure needed to make it all work.

The flip side of these investment is that while it can cost more in the short term, it could allow for faster growth in the long term, something WB needs since the top line has been rather flattish in recent years. Both AI search and the recommendation engine, for instance, have a lot of potential by increasing engagement to drive growth. It is possible WB is currently in a transition phase where the bottom line is reduced at first in order for the top line to grow faster, which will then drive the bottom line higher.

Why AI could have a lot of potential for WB

If WB is to succeed to boosting growth like it needs to, then AI is likely to be the main reason as it offers the best opportunities to do so. Recent developments in the AI world have revealed encouraging growth prospects WB could utilize. For instance, WB started out with text messages in its early years as the Chinese counterpart to what was Twitter, but the WB of the future could rely more heavily on video than it does text.

A number of AI tools have recently become available that can generate videos from scratch, which have really impressed users for their ability to generate realistic videos. Seedance 2.0 has stood out, but there are others. This is something that did not exist before, but which could cause users to flock to the platform to, for instance, share their AI-generated videos on WB. WB may not be growing all that fast at the moment, but with the way AI is opening up new capabilities that users did not have before, and with WB making the investments needed to take advantage, it could be different in the future.

Investor takeaways

WB has not done well recently and not without reason. The stock market has been getting hit by fighting in the Middle East, which has weighed on stocks. It does not help that WB released a soft report that featured reduced earnings. The dividend, which WB can count as among its strongest attributes, was also reduced.

However, the case for long WB has a number of arguments in its favor. The dividend even at the reduced rate is still better than most. The stock is oversold after the recent decline and close to a support level, which makes for a good time to enter in on WB. WB is virtually debt free with more cash than debt and a healthy balance sheet.

The income statement is more of an issue. While FY2025 saw an increase in GAAP earnings, this was the result of gains from investments and other income not related to operations. In terms of other metrics, the bottom line got worse. WB remains a profitable business overall with relatively high margins, but the decrease in operating income is something that must be reversed. This became even more so in the final quarter of the fiscal.

Nonetheless, there is reason for optimism. The decrease in earnings seems to be related to the investments WB has made to make the business grow faster. This includes a number of growth initiatives like AI search and a recommendation engine. The goal of all these initiatives, which tend to revolve around the use of AI, is to draw more people to the platform and/or get them to spend more time on the platform.

If this happens, revenue should grow faster than it has in recent years, which should help profits in the long run. In the short term, it will result in a hit to earnings as WB needs to open the purse and it may take time for growth initiatives to yield results. But it will be worth it if the result is faster growth for WB, something that is probably WB’s greatest weakness with its rather flattish growth.

I am therefore bullish on WB with everything stated earlier in mind. The recent earnings pressure is not something to be ignored, but WB deserves a chance at showing it can boost growth with its various growth initiatives. While earnings will get reduced as a consequence short term, it will be well worth it in my opinion if the result is a WB that becomes more dynamic with faster growth.

data.title: SoFi: Bulls Are Overlooking The Biggest Risk In The Story
data.description:
Back in January, I rated SoFi Technologies (SOFI) as a hold, as I was uncertain about the capital allocation strategy after the $3B raised across the July and December equity rounds.

The stock is down nearly 40%.

In the last earnings call, CFO Chris Lapointe didn't specify how they plan to spend the $3B. That said, his following remarks could be an indication that a broad acquisition spree is unlikely (which was my main concern heading into the Q4 print):

There's a lot of opportunities out there. I would say more than we've ever seen in eight years here. But I'd also say the bar is really high for us. When I say really high, we've looked at dozens and dozens of things, some of which were for sale, some of which were interesting to us. What we're prioritizing are things that can accelerate our growth versus the time it would take to build it ourselves.

Since my last coverage, I have been seeing a wave of contrarian investors and analysts who feel quite optimistic about the solid double-digit growth of the company.

In fact, the Street itself is projecting two more quarters of 30%+ revenue growth, followed by another two quarters with mid to high 20% growth.

That said, I prefer to remain a contrarian here and reiterate my hold rating. To be clear, the rationale has very little to do with valuation. In fact, I am not spooked by the 25x forward P/E multiple, even though it's significantly above the median in the financials sector.

What puts me off is the current macro backdrop, coupled with the earnings exposure of the company to its personal loan portfolio. My concern is that the current macroeconomic environment is pressuring the lower-end consumer.

The war in the Middle East is just half of the story. In fact, as I'm about to show in this article, there are early signs (very early, I have to admit) of leaving behind the highs of a credit cycle in personal loans.

My view could be wrong if the Strait of Hormuz reopens within the next few weeks, and the Fed starts cutting interest rates, starting in June. I believe there is a high chance for these two events to happen within that timeframe, which is the main reason I am not rating SoFi with a sell rating.

Below, I discuss more.

A Broad-Based Selloff In Credit Services

Let's put SoFi's selloff into context. The company is operating within the credit services industry, inside the broader financial services sector.

Take a look at the 3-month returns of the constituents within this industry:

The predominance of red tells me something: part of the selloff is driven by a risk-off move, external to the company.

Why?

Trump's call for a one-year 10% cap on credit-card interest rates starting January 20. I remember that day very well, as I was looking at the financials sector, and I could see a lot of red tiles in the heatmap visual above coming from stocks like Synchrony, Bread Financial, and Capital One falling between 8% and 11%. In fact, even Visa and Mastercard slipped 1.8% each.

Funny enough, by January 20, the overhang still had not cleared. In fact, the 10% cap is still not implemented today, March 31. Overall, back in January, analysts doubted the cap could be imposed without Congress, and I lean on the same view.

That said, a headline risk is a risk nonetheless, so one should consider this downside catalyst in their risk assessment for any company within the credit services industry.

A Few Headlines That Raised My Eyebrow

On March 30, Reuters reported that Robinhood and SoFi both pitched for roles in the retail distribution of a potential SpaceX IPO share sale in the U.S.

For reference, it is well known that SpaceX is targeting a late-2026 IPO, with valuations that go as far as $1T after the xAI merger. In fact, some sources are floating figures as high as $1.5T.

However, it appears that E*Trade was in talks to lead the IPO, and some sources suggest that SpaceX may be considering cutting Robinhood and SoFi out, though discussions are still ongoing.

It's a long shot, however, if SpaceX finally decides to lead its IPO with SoFi, that could be a headline to turn around the pessimism surrounding this stock.

Speaking of pessimism, it seems that Muddy Waters’ SoFi short campaign is gaining momentum after a recent short thesis published on March 30. That comes on top of their March 21 and March 17 reports.

What I found particularly interesting after these short reports was the reaction from the CEO. On March 17, CEO Anthony Noto filed a Form 4 showing an open-market purchase of 28,900 shares at a weighted-average price of $17.3189.

That's equivalent to a $500k million purchase. Not bad, although the stock is down 13.6% since his insider buying activity.

On top of that, he was the only one who bought in March. I would have loved to see a cluster of insider buying activity this month. Instead, one of the EVPs sold 3% of his position on March 20:

Why I'm Not Buying The Dip

It seems that I am not the only one. Back on March 19, Wells Fargo initiated coverage on SoFi with an equal weight rating, citing a price target of $19.

Back in January, BofA maintained an underperform rating, citing limited upside at the current multiple even after SoFi’s capital raise (a topic I discussed in my last coverage).

My discontent is with the high concentration of unsecured personal loans.

Let me explain why.

Personal loans represent balance sheet exposure. Therefore, any credit deterioration can hit revenue, fair-value marks, and capital generation.

My concern is that the consumer is now being pressured by high gas prices due to the Hormuz blockade. In fact, recent headlines like this one make me sweat:

If Hormuz is not reopened, the U.S. consumer will have to deal with higher gas prices for longer. For the consumers at the lower end of the K-shaped curve, that could mean defaulting on some of their loan payments.

In the last earnings release, personal-loan annualized charge-offs increased to 2.80% in Q4 from 2.60% in Q3. On top of that, the weighted-average annual default assumption on personal loans increased to 4.46% from 4.33% in the prior quarter.

As seen above, there is a big gap in that metric vs the student loan portfolio.

Again, it's the headlines that make me sweat. I include below one from February:

It seems that subprime loans are growing in the U.S., with some reports suggesting that 26.4 million consumers carried those loans as of the end of December last year, up from 24.5 million a year earlier.

On top of that, the New York Fed’s February 2026 household-debt update showed student-loan stress worsening, with 9.6% of balances 90+ days delinquent.

Even though I want to believe that SoFi has a very healthy personal loan base, these headlines only deteriorate the investor confidence in the credit services industry. Therefore, I don't see how the company will be able to turnaroud the poor sentiment surrounding its stock.

Looking Ahead

Since the highs in November last year, the stock has been down over 50%. That said, valuation still looks stretched. As I'm typing, investors are paying today 25x for the company's next year's earnings. For reference, the median of the financials sector is 9.6x.

Overall, aside from a long shot to participate in SpaceX's upcoming IPO, I don't see any compelling catalyst that would turn around the pessimism surrounding this stock (and also, surrounding the credit services industry).

Overall, over the last 3-months, I noticed a broader risk-off move in this industry, and given the recent headlines and the current pressures of higher gas prices for lower-end consumers, I am increasingly concerned.

From a technical perspective, the next support level appears to be at $10. That's a $5 haircut from today's $15.1 share price.

Overall, I prefer to remain on the sidelines until the sentiment in this industry improves, likely in the second half of this year, if (big if) Hormuz reopens.

data.title: Don't Be Fooled By The Average: IBHF's Real Yield Story
data.description:
The iShares iBonds 2026 Tеrm High Yield and Incоme ETF (IBHF) is a spеcific type of bond ETF. Unlikе traditional bond ETFs that roll оver their hоldings indefinitely, IBHF has a fixеd mаturity dаte. All hоldings mаture within thе samе calеndar yеar, aftеr which thе fund is liquidаted and rеturns cаpital to investors — similar to hоlding аn individual bоnd directly. This structurе makes thеm attrаctive to invеstors whо wаnt the divеrsification of а fund but the prеdictability of а fixеd mаturity. iShаres iBоnds 2026 Term High Yield and Income ETF was issued on 11/10/2020. At the time of writing, the fund has close to $1.04 billion in assets under management. Its management fee is close to 0.35% (expense ratio 0.35%). The 5-year total return on NAV is close to 4.57%, and the 3-year total return is around 8.67%. As of 03/27/2026, there are 44,660,000 shares outstanding.

Holdings

IBHF has 241 holdings at the time of writing. The weighted average coupon of all is around 4.27%, and the effective duration is close to 0.38 years. The largest part of the fund is 16.07% in the "Consumer Cyclical" sector, 12.89% in the "Finance Companies," and 10.77% in "Communications."

Almost all of the holdings (approximately 96.84%) have a maturity between 0 and 1 year, as you can see in the picture below. The weighted average maturity of IBHF is close to 0.39 years. 2.64% of the fund is in cash and derivatives.

In the next screenshot, the credit quality of the fund is shown. The biggest percentages are in BBB-rated (34.02%) and BB-rated (45.79%). 7.56% of the holdings are B-rated, and 8.58% are CCC-rated.

And here are the top 10 holdings of the fund:

IBHF's portfolio has an average weighted YTM of ~7.54%, which looks attractive for a short-term instrument with a maturity of 2026. Here we come to the main thesis of the article. This percentage is actually not 'real' - it is heavily distorted by a handful of holdings. In the following table, we have divided the ratio of holdings by the respective yield.

You can see for yourself that the largest part of the fund - close to 67%, or 161 holdings (with а total weight 33%) are with a yield to maturity below 5%. 22% are with yield to maturity between 5-6% аnd a total weight of 36.90%. The rest, 11%, are with yields above 6%. Only 3% of the fund has a yield to maturity above 15%. Below are shown all holdings of IBHF - you can see how the high-yield ones stand out significantly:

Thе mеdiаn weighted YTM is only аround 5.46%. Literаlly 70.9% of the pоrtfoliо carries a YTM bеlоw 6%, аnd 80.5% of the аssets are cоncentratеd in the 4–7% range, with a rеal averаge YTM of just 5.28%. Only 10 cоmpanies with а YTM аbove 10% hаve a wеight of approximately 8.5% аnd аlone cоntribute 2.54% of the tоtal 7.54% YTM, оr сlose to 33.7% оf the entirе avеrage YTM cоmеs from lеss thаn 9% of the pоrtfolio.

In the chart are shown the high-yield holdings that falsely raise the average YTM:

Many of them are with "Junk" credit ratings and are trading below par, even thоugh thеir mаturity is оnly mоnths аwаy—thе mаrket is еxprеssing sеrious dоubt thаt thеy will pаy оut thеir full vаluе.

But this is only a small part of the fund. In reality, 80% of IBHF's holdings are safer, with yields to maturity of between 4% and 7%, and are with higher credit ratings. With a real YTM of 5.28%, compared to the 6-month treasury yield, the spread is only close to 1.2% — for a high-yield fund with credit risk, this is a very tight compensation.

Since IBHF effectively behaves like a bond but carries a real average YTM of around 5%, investors may want to consider alternatives — investment-grade baby bonds trading below par, which offer higher yields with lower credit risk:

Conclusion

8.5% of the IBHF portfolio is 'dirty' and artificially inflates the average YTM by almost 2 full percentage points. The investor is buying something that looks like 7.54% but is actually 5.1% YTM. If yоu want to take risks and you are an investor who consciously wants distressed bonds, yоu cаn buy Hughеs Satellite or Baffinland directly. If yоu don't wаnt to risk, yоu're better off bеtting on аnоther ETF, аs there аre many аlternatives on thе mаrket for over 5%.

data.title: No, The Fed Isn't Going To Raise Rates
data.description:
Amidst the looming energy crisis that the U.S.-Iran War has hit the market, analysts galore are evoking the dreaded word “stagflation.” They fear that high oil prices, driven by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz by the Iranian military, will cause a double-whammy scenario in the economy where growth slows and inflation rises.

This kind of scenario emerges during supply shocks, where the limiting factor for the growth component—in this case, crude oil—is not demand-based. Persistent and prolonged shortages cause strange behavior for prices, which usually rise even as the real (inflation-adjusted) output and purchasing power of market participants actively falls.

At the extreme, past supply shocks have caused the U.S. to go into prolonged recession while experiencing double-digit inflation. It makes sense that historical parallels around the supply of oil reignite the fears of a repeat.

Here's the decade of real stagflation, from 1972 to 1982, where both inflation and unemployment hit double digits twice. Note that at all points in this chart, both inflation and unemployment are higher than current levels.

So of course, you get this next kind of comparison sent around with little context, which no doubt raises the blood pressure of many investors. They fear current policy—fiscal, monetary, or both—will cause us to return to such conditions.

But I consider this kind of thing an exercise in pareidolia at best. The underlying conditions are nothing alike.

Stagflation Fears Are Overblown

I'm not here to say that economic fears are unfounded, because they rightfully are on many fronts. But there is a distinction between higher-than-expected inflation and stagflation.

On one hand, yes, the Fed has been unable to get inflation below 2% for the last 63 months. That's true, even when we give them the benefit of the doubt by going with their preferred metric, core PCE.

What matters is the margin between the target and actual rates and their directionality. Inflation hasn't meaningfully ticked up, even after the institution of global tariffs—although the inflation we did see was clearly tariff-driven, as it was largely coming from goods price inflation, which had been a deflationary force for the price index before Liberation Day. Despite the policy bumps in the road, the disinflation trend hasn't stopped when we look at a larger picture.

It's unclear how long we have until tariff policy fully bakes into prices, and there is rightful fear as well that more inflation is to come. I don't foresee any return to above 4% on the CPI.

What will ultimately matter, apart from keeping CPI depressed during demand-driven periods, is growth. Growth matters a lot for defeating stagflationary environments because it necessarily needs high unemployment. This is what made 2022 not a stagflationary event, because unemployment was abating at the time. So while prices rose and the consumer took a hit in that regard, it was made up for in wage gains and higher overall employment.

I recommend this white paper from Hudson Capital, “Tech Trumps Tariffs,” to folks who want to read up more on the forces at play on the growth side and why they stand a chance at outrunning inflationary pressures. They discuss the effects of AI on the labor market and how productivity increases could create growth that offsets all or even more than the inflationary pressures of protectionist trade policy.

But my major argument against the past repeating so easily is that the conditions are different this time around. Namely, crude oil production is in a very different place. Instead of declining like it was throughout the 1970s, we've never produced more oil than we do today.

The supply shocks that caused the 1970s inflation crises (on top of decoupling from gold at the same time) are far less of a worry today. The U.S. was a net importer of crude oil back then, and much of it came from the Middle East. The Arab Oil Embargo was the weaponization of that circumstance as a geopolitical tool.

But today the U.S. is a net exporter of oil and oil products. Of what it does import, 70% comes from Canada and never travels through any strait or canal.

We can see this dynamic manifesting in regionality in oil pricing, where Hormuzian oil carries a 50-100% premium over oil from Texas or Kansas.

That's not to say that oil prices, and thus gas, jet fuel, and all things oil is embedded in (read as: everything), won't rise some if disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz drag on. The severity is the question.

The supply shock won't harm America as much as it did in the past; plus, this time, the nations that led the Arab Oil Embargo are on the U.S.'s side of this conflict, and they're working hard to reroute as much oil as possible around Hormuz.

The Fed's Path Is Murky, But It's Not Up

My favorite comments on the subject come straight from Fed Chair Powell at his last presser, the penultimate one he will be sitting chair for (though he will likely stay in as chair pro tem until Warsh is confirmed):

...stagflation. Yeah, so as I mentioned, there’s tension between the two goals, right? The upward risk for inflation and the downward risk for employment. And that puts us in a different situation. You know, when we use the term “stagflation,” I always have to point out that that was a 1970s term at a time when unemployment was in double figures, and inflation was really high, and the misery index was super high—add them together, you get the misery index. And that’s not the case right now. We actually have unemployment really close to longer-run normal, and we have inflation that’s, you know, 1 percentage point above that. So calling that “stagflation,” it’s not—I would reserve the term “stagflation” for a much more serious set of circumstances. That is not the situation we’re in. What we have is some tension between the goals, and we’re trying to manage our way through it. It’s a very difficult situation, but it’s nothing like what they faced in the 1970s, and that’s—I reserve stagflation for that—the word—for that period. Maybe that’s just me.

Naturally, one must be suspicious of public figures and watch what they do, not just what they say. Thankfully, the Fed just showed us a few things.

There is the consideration that the Fed just raised their long-term guidance on the Fed Funds Rate, but they have essentially said that we may already be close to, if not at, a terminal rate. This is different from saying that we need to go higher. The Fed has been fairly clear that nobody wants to go higher, and the last three meetings had no votes for raising rates. The folks who did vote for raises in the past two years have all been regional presidents, who rotate between meetings. The governors have all trended toward dissenting lower, not higher.

Of course, this would change if we saw meaningfully higher inflation, but for now it's clear that the view is dovish and not hawkish.

Traders are pricing in something different, and here's where fear is showing through in market consensus. Since the start of the war with Iran, the odds of rate cuts have shattered.

The Fed meeting mid-month sealed the deal, and now traders are expecting the raised terminal rate to be below the ending 2027 rate; effectively, that means that market consensus is for the Fed to cut twice over the course of a year and three quarters.

When asked about inflation expectations and what a supply shock could look like, Powell has given fairly tame answers. His answer at a recent, and based on the dot plot issued at the last Fed meeting, which came two weeks into the war. By the time the crude oil benchmark had already seen a high of $120/barrel, it was simple:

The Fed doesn't see an oil shock as a long-term impairment to their goals, and they see no short-term use for their tools. It puts them in a bind where they would prefer to “wait and see” instead of overreact if oil prices don't hold this high into the medium term (months).

It was clear from his most recent remarks, which rallied treasury notes, that the Fed intends to wait this out until oil and gas prices start to show in embedded ways (when they leak into core PCE), which it may never do.

If the Strait of Hormuz is reopened within the next month and flows resume, we should expect the core figures not to move too much. Western oil reserves are still ample. Non-core figures are likely to rise as gasoline prices directly impact it, but the total impact is unclear. We could expect a ~0.5% increase for every $1 the national gas price rises on average. At the current moment, we might expect March CPI, all else equal, to come in ~0.5-0.75 higher than February's.

While that could rattle markets, I believe that traders will expect it to print hot but be temporary. If the Fed is right and it's a temporary disruption, then they should be looking through it.

Monetary policy tools like raising rates will simply take too long to take effect. By the time demand-side levers like interest rates reverberate through the economy, the oil shock might be completely over, but the economic impact on businesses wouldn't be. They'd prefer to play it safe in this case.

Conclusion

The Fed is in a tough spot due to the ongoing oil supply shock, but conditions are very different today than in the 1970s, and fears that we will experience stagflation are largely overblown. Unemployment is sitting around its average, and inflation is above target by a point, though it may rise in the short term if oil stays elevated. The ingredients needed for stagflation are not in place, especially considering the productivity boost that AI looks likely to bring with it. Inflation, if it comes, won't become stagflation if growth remains intact.

I find myself agreeing with the Dallas Fed on this one; this ends in one of three ways: infinite abundance, T-800s, or a ~0.2% real increase in the long-term GDP trend. My money is on the last option, which would absolutely defeat a stagflationary environment even if inflation stays elevated above 3% for another few years.

Of course, much of this analysis of the situation hinges on the U.S.-Iran War not dragging out for the long term. While the administration has given mixed timelines, they all seem rather short, concluding before May. Perhaps that is too rosy a view of the situation, but it is too difficult for me to give anything more conclusive on that front.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Pricing The Shock: Oil, Volatility And Portfolio Resilience
data.description:
By Sam Peters, CFA

Calibrating Surprise Through Oil

The current conflict with Iran and the effective closing of the Strait of Hormuz is the year’s first big “information-rich” event—a surprise that introduces a shock of uncertainty the market must price over time as new information reduces uncertainty. The defining characteristic of these events is a large spike in volatility, which will ultimately result in price discovery at a level that reflects the new information.

Information-rich events are typically good tests of portfolio construction and often require some level of adaptation. They also create new opportunities, typically in the form of exploitable risk premiums and behavioral errors resulting from overreactions or underreactions. Our portfolio construction has held up as designed following some recent portfolio adaptations, and, although we are observing some elevation in risk premiums, the market’s underreaction to changes in energy and commodity markets offers attractive forward return opportunities.

The best way to measure surprise events is with volatility. With the latest Hormuz crisis, the best gauge is the oil volatility index (OVX), which has registered the second-largest oil volatility event on record, surpassed only by the demand collapse during COVID when oil briefly hit negative levels in Spring 2020. At its peak, the OVX reached 120, implying daily moves in oil price of roughly 8%. This level of volatility is not sustainable and has already begun to fall as uncertainty has waned (Exhibit 1).

The critical unknown variable is the duration of the Strait closure. This is important, as the closure has the potential to be the largest oil supply shock in history. Currently, an estimated eight million barrels per day (mmb/d) are offline, according to the International Energy Agency, largely a function of storage filling behind the Strait. Analysts estimate the supply shock could approach or exceed 10 mmb/d within weeks, a disruption that cannot be offset by Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) releases—which could optimally cover roughly four mmb/d — or a quick supply response.

Exhibit 1: Hormuz Closure Results in Historic Volatility

If this situation is not resolved within the next couple of weeks, global oil inventories will decline toward historic lows, and prices would need to rise to induce demand destruction. The critical level is when global consumption exceeds 4% of global GDP, roughly $150 oil, which would raise recession probabilities materially, increasing U.S. recession odds from approximately 20% pre-Iran to the roughly 31% we are currently observing.

The market is not pricing a drawn-out crisis. Both the oil forward curve and volatility curve remain in steep backwardation—reflecting a sharp spike in spot prices with expectations for normalization later in 2026. However, the forward curve continues to move higher as the closure persists, signaling oil is unlikely to revert to $60 levels but will instead settle higher depending on the timing of a resolution.

Positioning to Persevere Through Volatility

We are evaluating three scenarios as events unfold:

  1. Near-term resolution (~30% probability, declining): Strait reopens within two weeks, cumulative drawdown of supply of 20 million to 40 million barrels, minimal structural damage, and oil settles in the $70–$75 range in the second half of the year.
  2. Extended disruption (~60% probability): Closure lasts four to eight weeks, drawing 300 million to 500 million barrels, exhausting the SPR, and pushing inventories to multiyear lows. Oil stabilizes in the $80–$95 range into 2027 with signs of structural damage.
  3. Tail event (~10% probability): Conflict extends into early summer or beyond, resulting in structural damage, continued closure, and cumulative draws exceeding one billion barrels. Oil spikes toward $200, forcing demand destruction, with prices remaining above $100 for an extended period as markets rebalance.

Probability-adjusted outcomes suggest oil prices settle above $70 but quickly escalate as a function of time.

These dynamics are increasingly central to portfolio construction. We have been adapting the portfolio to be more resilient to volatility spikes, which have recently been characterized by extremely fast ascents and equally fast normalization, with limited expansion in exploitable risk premiums. Our approach has been to reduce exposure to high-volatility, high-correlation names that consume a disproportionate part of our risk budget, lowering downside during spikes and allowing us to play offense more quickly.

This positioning has worked as intended. During the recent episode, the VIX reached 28—a one standard deviation move—and the portfolio declined roughly 80 basis points relative on a gross basis, in line with or better than peers, without requiring defensive repositioning. In fact, we added to select names we believe are well-positioned when the crisis abates.

At the same time, the opportunity set is evolving alongside commodity markets. We believe market leadership will continue to broaden and that the ongoing bull market in real assets will be reinforced by this crisis. Our portfolio reflects this with an active overweight of over 800 basis points in energy and materials. One of the key benefits of this positioning is that commodity return tails are symmetric—downside from demand shocks like COVID and upside from supply shocks like Hormuz—providing diversification against the structural left-tail skew from long equity exposure.

Energy fundamentals are also improving meaningfully, with cash flow and earnings estimates seeing positive revisions. We expect energy to continue to lead the market in 2026. However, the most constructive outcome for energy equities is a quicker resolution, as energy stocks typically perform better when prices stabilize at higher levels—the most likely current outcome -- rather than during acute price spikes.

If stagflation risks become more embedded, energy and health care have historically been two of the few areas of outperformance. We have used recent healthcare weakness to add exposure while remaining fully positioned in energy. Despite this, risk premiums remain compressed, and we are still positioned defensively relative to history, maintaining dry powder to act more aggressively when we are better compensated for risk.

Sam Peters, CFA is a Portfolio Manager at ClearBridge Investments and co-manages the Value Equity Strategy and the All Cap Value Strategy, and has over 30 years of investment experience. Sam earned a BA in economics from the College of William & Mary and an MBA from the University of Chicago. He received the CFA designation in 1997.

data.title: ClearBridge Dividend Strategy Portfolios Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: 2025 marked the third consecutive year of this AI-driven cycle. The market-cap-weighted S&P 500 Index rose 17.9% in the year, while the equal-weighted S&P 500 Index gained just 11.4%. The Magnificent Seven, this market's nucleus, rose 24.9% in 2025. While performance comparisons will vary based on the time period, cap-weighted and equal-weighted indexes have produced similar returns over long periods of time.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in consumer staples, a health care overweight and IT underweight proved beneficial.
  • Detractors: Quarterly underperformance was driven primarily by stock selection in the communication services, IT, materials and real estate sectors.
  • Outlook: We anticipate the AI debate will carry over in 2026 and we will continue to participate in AI in a measured and disciplined way. Yet, as other investors continue to myopically focus on AI, we expect to find additional idiosyncratic opportunities in overlooked corners of the market. We believe these seeds will bear fruit in the years to come.

Performance Review

  • The S&P 500 managed a 2.7% gain in the fourth quarter, while the Strategy captured roughly half of that.
  • On the plus side, consumer staples holdings such as Nestle, Coca-Cola and Unilever and a health care overweight shined as concerns of a tech bubble grew.
  • On the downside, we are underweight Alphabet and Apple, two Magnificent Seven stocks that performed well, and overweight Oracle, which gave back some of its large gains from earlier in 2025. Increasing competitive dynamics in the wireless industry also weighed on T-Mobile.
  • 2025 marked the third consecutive year of this AI-driven cycle. The market-cap-weighted S&P 500 Index rose 17.9% in the year, while the equal-weighted S&P 500 Index gained just 11.4%. The Magnificent Seven, this market's nucleus, rose 24.9% in 2025. Since ChatGPT launched three years ago, the cap-weighted S&P 500 has delivered nearly twice the gains of its equal weighted peer. The Magnificent Seven, meanwhile, has surged a staggering 332%.
  • While performance comparisons will vary based on the time period, cap-weighted and equal-weighted indexes have produced similar returns over long periods of time.
  • In 2025, and throughout the last three years, the ClearBridge Dividend Strategy has enjoyed healthy gains, in part due to its investments in AI participants like Alphabet, Broadcom, Meta Platforms, Microsoft and Oracle.
  • We have not gained as much as the cap-weighted S&P 500, our benchmark, however, as we maintain diversification to prevent the portfolio from becoming overly concentrated.
  • Market conditions in 2025 provided opportunities in companies we have long followed and admired such as: Automatic Data Processing (a fourth-quarter addition), Inditex, L3Harris, Marsh & McLennan, Old Dominion Freight Lines and TE Connectivity. We also took advantage of situations in select existing holdings to significantly increase our positions in Air Products, Exxon Mobil and Union Pacific. All represent high-quality franchises in attractive industries underwritten based on reasonable cash returns. It is an eclectic bunch, sourced from bottom-up analysis, not a trend-following, top-down approach.
  • Given our approach to investing — conservative, diversified and risk-averse — AI is not central to our portfolio. Indeed, no one sector or trend will ever be central to the ClearBridge Dividend Strategy.
  • In momentum-driven, concentrated markets, diversification inherently impacts relative performance comparisons. Of course, it also limits losses when that trade, inevitably, unwinds.

Outlook

  • We anticipate the AI debate will carry over in 2026 and we will continue to participate in AI in a measured and disciplined way. Yet, as other investors continue to myopically focus on AI, we expect to find additional idiosyncratic opportunities in overlooked corners of the market. We believe these seeds will bear fruit in the years to come. The Strategy, meanwhile, continues to trade at a significant discount to the broader market while exhibiting robust dividend growth on average. With market concentration and valuations at or near all-time highs, we remain committed to diversification and valuation discipline. These principles have served investors well in the past; we believe they will continue to serve us well in the years ahead.

data.title: April Dogs Of The Dow: One To Buy
data.description:
Foreword

While most of this collection of Dow Industrials is too pricey and reveals only skinny dividends, April (again) finds only Verizon (VZ), living up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share price and it is also ‘safer’ because it has more-than-enough free-cash-flow to support its dividends.

In late February, 2024 Dow Jones replaced the low priced high yield dividend payer Walgreens, with Amazon, a high-priced non-dividend payer. More recently, in November, 2024, Intel was replaced by Nvidia and Dow Inc was supplanted by Sherwin Williams.

With renewed downside market pressure up to 83.3% all ten of the top ‘safer’ April Dow dividend dogs could become elite fair-priced buys with annual yield (from $1K invested) meeting or exceeding their single share prices.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced Dow Dogs in Actionable Conclusion 21 near the mid-point of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 34.45% To 65.35% Net Gains From Top-Ten Dow Dogs By April 2027

Three of ten top dividend-yielding Dow dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this April, 2027 yield-based forecast for Dow dogs, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 30% accurate.

Dividend-returns estimated from $1,000.00 invested in each of the ten highest-yielding Dow stocks and one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, produced the 2026-27 data pointsmbelow. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten likely profit-generating trades for April 2027 were:

Microsoft (MSFT) was projected to net $653.65, based on the median of target price estimates from 57 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 10% greater than the market as a whole.

NVIDIA (NVDA) was projected to net $591.24, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 60 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 133% greater than the market as a whole.

Salesforce (CRM) was projected to net $527.34, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 54 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 29% over the market as a whole.

Nike (NKE) was projected to net $481.92, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 39 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 27% over the market as a whole.

The Boeing Co (BA) was projected to net $413.47, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 26 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this 14% more than the market as a whole.

United Health Group (UNH) was projected to net $409.74, based on the median of target price estimates from 29 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 59% less than the market as a whole.

Disney (DIS) was projected to net $404.20, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 31 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 43% over the market as a whole.

Amazon.com (AMZN) was projected to net $401.36 based on the median of target prices estimated by 68 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 40% over to the market as a whole.

Visa (V) was projected to net 348.89, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 38 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 22% under the market as a whole.

International Business Machines Corporation (IBM) was projected to net $344.49 based on the median of target prices estimated by 21 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 27% under to the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 45.76% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten Dow Index stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 19% over the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks are dividend dogs when they show three key qualities: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, and (2) their prices fall. Therefore, (3) yield (dividend/price) grows higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection become valued as ”dividend dogs." More specifically, they may be best-named "underdogs,” owing to their contrarian nature.

30 Dow Dogs By 1Yr Total Returns

30 Dow Dogs By Analyst Target Gain Estimates

The April, 2026 Dow 30 By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top April Dow Dividend Stocks By Yield Ranged 2.43% To 5.63%

Top ten Dow Dogs represented seven of eleven Morningstar sectors.

The lone communication services dog placed first, Verizon [1], to lead the pack. Three healthcare stocks placed second, sixth, and eighth: United Health [3], Merck (MRK) [5], and Amgen (AMGN) [7].

A lone energy stock placed third, Chevron (CVX) [3] and two consumer discretionary members placed fourth and sixth, NIKE (NKE) [4], and Home Depot (HD) [6],

Two April consumer staple stalwarts, placed fifth and tenth: Procter & Gamble (PG) [5], and Coca Cola Co (KO) [10].

Finally, one technology member placed eighth, International Business Machines Corporation [8], to complete the March top-ten Dogs of the Dow by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The graphic above shows the relative strengths of the top ten April Dow dogs by dividend and price as of market close 3/27/2026.

Nearly all top-ten Dow dogs show a less than ideal status because the prices of nine exceed the projected annual dividends from $1k invested in each.

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by just one of the five lowest priced Dogs of the Dow. Only Verizon, lived up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share prices.

Dow Index Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 3/27/26 for April

The above chart reveals thirty Dow dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

Two stocks in the Dow index pay no dividend and are, therefore, excluded from the calculation. For those that count, April’s least expensive dog of the Dow is Verizon $17.78 per dividend dollar and the most expensive is NVIDIA (NVDA) at $4,184.10 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Two of Twenty-Eight Dow Dividend Dogs Show No or Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

No, or a negative, gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividend, The April pair are Coca-Cola, and 3M. They are added to the two non-dividend payers considered unsafe to buy due to dividend yields equaling or exceeding free cash flow yields.

Two cut their dividends in the 2020 Ides of March depression times, Boeing, and The Walt Disney Co. One more Salesforce (CRM), snuck onto the Dow index without a dividend.

However, in March 2023, Salesforce declared its first-ever quarterly payout and Disney, in January, 2024 rejoined the dividend paying ranks. However four (two short on cash and two non-dividend payers, Amazon, and Boeing) cast a pall on the Dow by being stockholder unfriendly.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, TACO deals, wars, ICE, and impulse actions, too.

Price Drops, Dividend Increases or Rerates Could Get All Ten Dow Dogs Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, one in the top ten is already ideally priced. Beside Verizon, already in the ideal zone, nine more top-yield stocks are within $249 of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that these companies raise their dividends or split shares. Those options may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts, just watch and buy when your intended stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 32.61% To 65.34% Top Ten Dow Index Upsides To April, 2027; (32), One -5.28% Downsider Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates became another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 10.4% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 Dow Dogs To April, 2027

Ten-top Dow April updated yield (dividend / price) results, as verified by YCharts, produced the following results:

Dow top ten noted 3/27/26 by yield, (for April) represented six of eleven sectors. (Real Estate is not reported by the Dow and Utilities has its own Dow Index. Materials, Financials, and Industrials, also missed the top-ten cut.)

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Predicted 5 Lowest-Priced of the Ten Highest-Yield Dow Dogs (34) To Deliver 18.93% Vs. (35) 17.15% Net Gains by All Ten Come April, 2027

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Dow Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 10.4% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The second lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, NIKE Inc, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 48.19%.

The five lowest-priced Dow top-yield dogs for April were: Verizon; Nike; Cola-Cola; Merck; Procter & Gamble, with prices ranging from $50.31 to $142.71.

Five higher-priced Dow top-yield dogs as of March 27 were: Chevron; International Business Machines Corporation; UnitedHealth; Home Depot; Amgen, with prices ranging from $211.15 to $348.77.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the ongoing Fed, trade and war-fueled slide gains momentum through 2026, with the exception of a very few Techno, and (maybe) war materials stocks, look for at least half of the ten Dow Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or quite suddenly.

Afterword

Buy One ‘Safer' Dow April Dividend Dog and Watch Six More

Late March 2026, (for April) 19 of the 30 Dow Index were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 3/27/26 data showed one was IDEAL and fair-priced.

How Dow 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Could Produce A "Fair Price” Result For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that one dividend from $1K invested in our top ten 'safer' dividend Dow dogs conforms to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or less than) the price of a single share. That one is Verizon Communications still standing after passing the ‘safer’ dividend gauntlet.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called “fair price.”

A market correction of 83.29% is required to put all 10 “safer” survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors these companies to raise dividends or create stock splits to bring prices the dogcatcher ideal level. That’s a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much “good” elsewhere.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Dogs of the Dow updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

Footnote

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Dow dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations. Just remember Verizon In March 2026 and the six stand-by Dow dogs of the month: Cisco, Merck, Chevron, IBM, JNJ, JPMorgan Chase, and Amgen.
